episode.title: AeroVista 7-Division Overview - One ecosystem, shared direction

content.summary_short: What AeroVista is, why the seven divisions are peers, how HQ fits, and how real cross-division work combines learning, engineering, media, publishing, and brand execution.
content.summary_long: EP-002 gives the learner a practical company map. Instead of treating AeroVista as one product, it frames the organization as one platform with seven peer divisions plus HQ for strategy and cross-division alignment. The episode also grounds the model in real combinations a learner can understand quickly: ByteCast and training align naturally with Summit, repeatable tooling aligns with Nexus, publishing and research work align with Vespera, and shared presentation quality depends on Lumina and the rest of the ecosystem working together. Full scene-by-scene copy lives in docs/day1/EP-002_VOICEOVER.md.

slides.title: EP-002 - AeroVista 7-Division Overview
slides.goal: Set the scope: learn the ecosystem shape, not every file and product.
bullets: One ecosystem. Seven divisions. Shared direction.
The learner goal is orientation: what AeroVista is, why it exists, and how the major lanes fit together.
This episode turns the company from a vague brand umbrella into a usable mental map.

slides.title: What AeroVista is
slides.goal: See the company as one platform with multiple kinds of work.
bullets: AeroVista is not one app or one service. It is a multimedia and technology platform built around creative, technical, and operational lanes.
The business spans learning, tools, publishing, brand work, audio, interactive media, and real-world capture.
The useful Day 1 takeaway is simple: one business, many specialized lanes, shared direction.

slides.title: Mission, vision, and philosophy
slides.goal: Understand why the ecosystem feels broad by design.
bullets: Mission: build emotionally resonant, AI-enhanced entertainment, education, and immersive experiences.
Vision: become a connected creative ecosystem where gaming, publishing, music, digital media, and learning reinforce each other.
Philosophy: story first, humans plus AI, and integration instead of isolated silos.

slides.title: The seven divisions (peers + HQ)
slides.goal: Learn the structure rule that keeps the map honest.
bullets: Nexus, EchoVerse, SkyForge, Summit, Lumina, Vespera, and Horizon are peer divisions, not subfolders under one master lane.
AeroVista HQ sits at the center for strategy, brand oversight, and cross-division operations.
That parity matters because learners need to expect collaboration across peers, not a fake hierarchy.

slides.title: Nexus TechWorks
slides.goal: Recognize the engineering and systems lane.
bullets: Nexus covers apps, SaaS, automation, AI systems, architecture, and operational tooling.
If a workflow needs a repeatable system, platform shell, or technical backbone, Nexus is a natural builder home.
In the learner's world, Seed Builder and other repeatable tooling patterns make the Nexus lane easier to recognize.

slides.title: EchoVerse Audio
slides.goal: Understand the audio lane as both product and platform capability.
bullets: EchoVerse covers music, sound design, licensing, listening experiences, and collaborative audio workflows.
This lane is not just songs; it is audio as an experience layer and product surface.
When AeroVista turns sound into a reusable audience or platform experience, EchoVerse is part of that story.

slides.title: SkyForge Creative Studios
slides.goal: See the immersive and interactive lane clearly.
bullets: SkyForge is the home for games, interactive storytelling, AR, VR, and immersive worlds.
It represents the platform's move from content into playable or experiential spaces.
When AeroVista needs worldbuilding, play, and interactive narrative, SkyForge is a key engine.

slides.title: Summit Learning
slides.goal: Anchor the education lane and connect ByteCast back to the bigger company map.
bullets: Summit covers courses, learning systems, AI training, knowledge management, and structured education experiences.
ByteCast sits naturally in this learning lane because it teaches, checks understanding, and routes people into stronger proof.
This matters because the training system is not a side project; it belongs inside a company that treats learning as a first-class product.

slides.title: Lumina Creative Media
slides.goal: Understand the brand and presentation layer.
bullets: Lumina handles branding, presentation systems, marketing materials, launch polish, and audience-facing coherence.
It helps the ecosystem feel trustworthy and recognizable across many surfaces.
When work from multiple divisions has to read like one company, Lumina helps hold the line.

slides.title: Vespera Publishing
slides.goal: Recognize the structured writing and publishing lane.
bullets: Vespera covers books, formatted content, editorial flow, research packaging, and publishable written output.
It gives shape, order, and delivery to ideas that start as drafts or knowledge assets.
When a learning or story project needs durable written form, Vespera often joins the stack.

slides.title: Horizon Aerial & Visual
slides.goal: Remember that AeroVista reaches into real-world capture too.
bullets: Horizon covers drone work, aerial products, mapping, cinematography, and visual capture.
It gives the ecosystem a physical-world media arm instead of limiting everything to digital-only output.
That broadened capability helps explain why the overall platform feels larger than a software shop.

slides.title: Why HQ matters
slides.goal: See HQ as the strategic center, not a vague label.
bullets: HQ aligns strategy, brand, and cross-division operations so the platform moves as one business.
Its job is not to erase division identity; it is to keep shared direction from dissolving into drift.
The fast summary is: seven lanes, one platform, one strategic center.

slides.title: Cross-division collaboration
slides.goal: Treat collaboration as normal, not special-case.
bullets: Shared technology, shared components, billing, brand systems, and coordinated execution are part of the platform model.
A learning product can involve Summit for curriculum, Nexus for systems, and Vespera for structured written output.
A launch can combine Lumina for presentation, EchoVerse for audio, and Nexus or SkyForge for the technical surface.

slides.title: The visual mental map
slides.goal: Keep the map stable even when the project list changes.
bullets: Think AeroVista home first, then peer divisions, then apps, projects, and offers beneath each lane.
The learner does not need every filename; the learner needs the shape of the ecosystem and how the lanes relate.
Once that frame is stable, later tool- or project-level details stop feeling random.

slides.title: Why this matters to a learner
slides.goal: Connect company context to lower confusion later.
bullets: People get lost when they meet tools before they understand the organization behind them.
This map reduces that drift by showing which lane owns what kind of outcome.
It also prepares the learner to understand why later episodes care about doors, truth boundaries, and ownership.

slides.title: Quick recap
slides.goal: Lock the company map before Engage.
bullets: AeroVista is a unified platform, not one isolated product.
The seven divisions are peers, HQ aligns the system, and value comes from integration as much as specialization.
ByteCast lives inside a broader organization that combines learning, engineering, brand, publishing, audio, interactive work, and capture.

slides.title: Before you continue
bullets: Explain AeroVista in one sentence and name all seven divisions.
Name one division that naturally fits learning, one that fits tooling, and one that fits durable publishing output.
Next: EP-003 teaches where to go for which job once you know the company map.

items.text: Use Listen and move through the slide deck through Quick recap.
Pick three divisions and state one type of outcome each division is built to own.
Describe one realistic cross-division combination, such as learning plus tooling plus publishing.
Say aloud why division parity matters more than memorizing every current project.

choices: a unified creative and technology ecosystem
questions.explain: The company is framed as one connected platform with multiple specialized lanes.
Division parity is part of the organizational model.

choices: strategy, brand oversight, and cross-division operations
questions.explain: HQ aligns the business while divisions own their lanes.
Shared systems and coordinated work are structural, not incidental.

questions.q: Which combination best fits a ByteCast-like learning product with durable written output?
questions.explain: Learning, systems, and publishable structured content often combine across those lanes.

aerovista_about.summary: AeroVista is a unified multimedia and technology platform: seven peer divisions with AeroVista HQ for strategy, brand, and cross-division operations.

divisions.name: Nexus TechWorks
divisions.bread_and_butter: AI and technology: apps, SaaS, automation, architecture, cloud-backed systems.

divisions.name: EchoVerse Audio
divisions.bread_and_butter: Music, sound design, licensing, collaborative audio, and listening experiences.

divisions.name: SkyForge Creative Studios
divisions.bread_and_butter: Games, interactive storytelling, AR/VR, and immersive worlds.

divisions.name: Summit Learning
divisions.bread_and_butter: Courses, learning systems, AI training, and knowledge management.

divisions.name: Lumina Creative Media
divisions.bread_and_butter: Branding, design systems, social, marketing, and visual presentation.

divisions.name: Vespera Publishing
divisions.bread_and_butter: Books, formatting, research content, and publishing workflows.

divisions.bread_and_butter: Drone photography, aerial products, cinematography, mapping, and capture.
